title: What happens during a cavity wall insulation installation
subtitle: The full process from survey to completion inspection for a 1960s or 1970s house
excerpt: Cavity wall insulation fills the 50 to 75 mm air gap in the walls of houses built from around 1920 to 1995, replacing the air cavity with mineral wool fibre or EPS beads. The actual installation takes one day for a typical semi-detached house, but the process that surrounds it involves a pre-installation survey, a BBA or KIWA-approved system, and a completion inspection.
sourceNotes: gov.uk guidance on cavity wall insulation under the Boiler Upgrade Scheme and ECO4 eligibility criteria. CIGA (Cavity Insulation Guarantee Agency) guarantee conditions version 12. BBA Certificate for mineral wool cavity fill (Knauf Supafil, Isover cavity wall systems). PAS 2030 for installer requirements.

glossaryTerms.definition: A 25-year guarantee issued by the Cavity Insulation Guarantee Agency for cavity wall insulation installed by registered members using approved materials.
A wall construction with two parallel layers of masonry separated by an air gap, standard in UK houses built from the 1920s onwards.

content.text: A
cavity wall

with a 65 mm unfilled air gap has a U-value of around 1.5 W/m²K. After filling with mineral wool or EPS beads, that falls to 0.45 to 0.55 W/m²K. On a house with 80 m² of exposed cavity wall, the annual heating season saving is around 1,800 to 2,400 kWh, which at 6p per kWh (gas) is 108 to 144 pounds per year. The work itself takes a single working day on a standard semi-detached house.
The pre-installation survey

A surveyor from the installation company visits before the installation date. The survey confirms that the cavity is clear (no rubble, no bridging) and that the wall construction is suitable. The main checks are:
Borescope check through a drilled hole: cavity clear, wall ties sound, cavity width within the approved range for the chosen material.

Exposure category of the property. Houses in high-exposure locations (coastal, elevated, or in areas with more than 56 days per year of wind-driven rain) require a WDR-rated insulation system. In high-exposure locations, standard blown mineral wool is replaced by a closed-cell EPS bead bonded with an adhesive to prevent moisture bridging.
Wall condition. Cracked or porous masonry is identified and noted. Installation proceeds only after the installer confirms the wall is watertight.
Location of party wall between semi-detached properties. The installation must stop at the party wall line to avoid filling the neighbour's cavity without consent.
The installation day

The installer drills injection holes across each wall face in a grid pattern. The typical grid is 1.2 to 1.5 m horizontal spacing, at staggered intervals vertically. The holes are 28 to 32 mm diameter for blown mineral wool; 22 mm for EPS beads.

For blown mineral wool, the hose nozzle is inserted to the back of the cavity and the blowing machine pumps the material in under controlled air pressure. The operative watches the fill resistance at the nozzle to confirm even packing with no bridging on wall ties.

Once each hole is full, the nozzle comes out and the hole is plugged with mortar matched to the pointing colour. On modern smooth-face brickwork, plugs are left slightly proud and rubbed back after curing.
The installer checks a sample of plugged holes with a borescope to confirm the material has packed evenly to the correct density.
The CIGA guarantee
Installations by registered members using approved materials qualify for a
CIGA guarantee

of 25 years. The guarantee covers the removal and reinstatement of the insulation if it causes damp ingress that was not caused by a pre-existing defect. It does not cover damp ingress caused by poor wall maintenance. The guarantee document includes the installation date, the material used, and the area treated; keep this for the property file.
When the installation site matters

A Victorian terrace in Edinburgh differs from a 1980s estate house in Swindon. The Edinburgh terrace may have stone cavity construction with an irregular cavity width; the surveyor will flag this as unsuitable or specify a loose-fill material that tolerates varying cavity widths. A timber-framed house from the 1990s has no masonry cavity to fill; the same process does not apply. A house in a high-exposure coastal location should only receive a WDR-rated installation. In all three cases, the pre-installation survey separates a good decision from one that causes problems later.
Done well versus done badly

Done well, the installation is preceded by a borescope survey confirming a clear, wide-enough cavity; uses a WDR-rated material in high-exposure locations; leaves plugged holes colour-matched to the pointing; and comes with a 25-year CIGA guarantee document. Done badly, the cavity is filled without a proper survey in a high-exposure house with porous brickwork. Within two winters, moisture bridges from the outer leaf to the inner leaf through the fill material; damp patches appear on the internal wall surface. Remediation means removing the insulation, repairing the pointing, and re-filling with an appropriate material.

attrs.symptom: Damp patches appearing on internal walls after fill

content.text: Check the external pointing condition around the affected area. If the pointing is cracked or open, water is entering the outer leaf and bridging to the inner leaf via the fill. Repair the pointing and allow to dry; if the damp persists, contact the CIGA guarantee holder.

A thermal image during cold weather will show any areas of unfilled cavity as cold patches. This usually indicates a bridged or blocked section of cavity. Contact the installer; a CIGA guarantee allows for free remediation where the fill is incomplete.
